text: This is a video presentation of the laparoscopic management of pancreatic pseudocyst. The patient is a forty-three year old male with a seven month history of symptomatic pseudocyst developing after an episode of acute alcoholic pancreatitis. Both cysts were over ten centimeters in size and one in the head of the pancreas, the other one in the tail. The patient had obstructive jaundice and early satiety. On ERCP, the cysts were not communicated to the main duct. CT imaging depicts both cysts, which were not in close proximity to the stomach. For this and other reasons, we elected a management with a cystogenostomy. The proposed operation attempts to drain both cysts into the same rural limb while preserving the continuation of the GI tract. And the patient was supine in a lithotomy position with four ports that were used as depicted in the picture.

Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon exposes the cyst at the head of the pancreas by taking down the gastrocolic omentum, revealing its anterior and inferior surfaces. The cyst at the tail of the pancreas is similarly exposed by entering the lesser sac and dissecting it free from attachments. The stomach is mobilized and retracted cephalad to the anterior abdominal wall to enhance exposure for working with the cyst.

The surgeon uses ultrasound to confirm the absence of solid material within the pancreatic cyst. The small bowel is transected 40 centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, and the mesentery is lengthened using a vascular load for increased mobility. A retrocolic window is created in the transverse mesocolon, allowing the jejunal limb to be positioned flat in the dependent area of the cysts at the tail and head of the pancreas. The anastomosis is first created at the tail of the pancreas.

A small piece of the capsule is excised for intraoperative frozen section analysis, revealing no epithelial surface. The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to perform an intraotomy and creates an anastomosis with a thick green load from the jejunal limb into the tail of the pancreatic cyst. The cavity of the cyst shows pancreatic debris without solid components. The cyst-enterotomy is closed with an absorbable suture in a running fashion.

The surgeon directs attention to the cyst at the head of the pancreas, performing a cystal enterotomy and sending the capsule for frozen section analysis. A thick load is used to create an anastomosis between the rule limb and the head of the pancreas cyst, with an anchoring suture to reduce tension. The cistoenterotomy is closed with an absorbable suture. Subsequently, a jejunojejunostomy is performed using a GIA stapler, and the entereotomy is closed with a running microsuture.
The surgeon closes the defects in the transverse mesocolon and the jejuno-jejunostomy using absorbable threovacral sutures. A flat JP drain is placed in the surgical site.
